comments.title: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For those of you in the US, whatever the outcome this Tuesday (or this week, or in a few months...), may any craziness pass you by and you find fulfilling ways forward for you and yours.

To Share: Some time ago, open_space recommended I read How to Pray the Rosary and Get Reuslts by Brother ADA, and I've finally done so and written my thoughts up on it: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_how_to_pray_the_rosary_and_get_results

To Ask: I've recently been revisiting an exercise that tells me to "put my consciousness into X part of my body." I have an extraordinarily hard time feeling like "I" am anywhere except behind my eyes. Other than "keep practicing!" do you, JMG, or anyone in the commentariat have any tips on how to get better at this kind of thing?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

Any suggestions?

Because I can do the former alright, but the latter seems to be what these exercises mean, and has wholly eluded me thus far.

Cheers,

replies.content:
What you're calling "the perceiver" in me shifts fairly readily up and down the midline of my body; it's only behind my eyes when I'm looking at something. (Moving it to one side or the other takes more work) You may have tried this already, but closing your eyes and paying attention to some other sense might help.

That said, just putting your attention somewhere has many of the same effects.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
replies.content:
In general, part of the problem is that there are two aspects to shifting awareness in parts of the body -- one is attention to a place, the other is attention through a place. The first tends to get in the way of the second, in the sense that, in attending to the place one wishes to attend through, the first sort of attention tends to block or supersede the second, since "attending to" tends to be external, so to speak, to what is being attended-to.

Another way to describe the problem is that, if one's "star of consciousness" is habitually located in one place, *trying* to shift to to another merely affirms the habitual location of the executive function.

Empirically, people often have one favored or habitual location for the "star of consciousness" -- often in one of the three general traditional locations: in the head or behind the eyes, in the heart or solar plexus, and in the belly.

One useful way to sift the location of one's sense of presence is to do so without trying, that is, without bringing the habitual location into even more prominence. One can do this not by trying to push one's sense of presence around, but by expanding it from within. For example, by gradually building up a sense of presence that fills one's whole body, so that the sense of presence rests within the body as a whole. It's best to begin with presence in the tactile and kinesthetic realms, and simply maintain this whole body awareness for up to half an hour at a time, linking it to the breath, so that as one breathes in one feels the sense of awareness coming into somewhat more focus, and as one breathes out, one feels it somewhat dissipating, through the whole body. (Trying to do this by visualizing one's body is likely to be self-defeating: one wants to experience one's body from the inside, not from the outside.)

After one can do this readily, and maintain it for a while, one can then practice focusing on the concentration and dispersion of awareness through some particular location, eg, the palm of one or both hands. Then one can pull and push the concentration and dispersion farther into the body, to the location of the star of awareness, and allow the sense of presence to merge with the flow of awareness with the breath. Or one can do something like the Middle Pillar exercise, but without working on visualization: just attending to the flow of awareness around and through the body, without getting stuck on any particular spot.

People often move the focus of awareness without explicitly recognizing what they are doing. For example, someone who uses tools that require perception as well as action (an anatomist using a probe to explore some internal structure, for example, or a calligrapher, or sculptor) may find themselves being aware through their hands, or even through the tool or instrument itself. This kind of re-focusing is often fluid and subtle enough that it doesn't become explicit. One just gets the hang of doing it.

Smaragdoplitanus

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
replies.content:
Hi Jeff,
I used to live very dissociated. Two things have helped me develop the capacity to put my consciousness anywhere in my body:

1) learning to send my breath/ breathe into to any part of my body. Obviously, that is more of an energetic exercise and it really helped me expand my awareness of every part of my body.

2) practicing a moving meditation practice like 5Rhythms which is designed to cultivate awareness and experience of every part of the body.

There is also a meditation technique I've found helpful for this. You begin by visualizing a ring resting on the top of your head and cultivate awareness of that part of your head by noticing what you sense there. Then you move the ring down a little further again feeling awareness in that part of your head. Keep moving your imaginary ring slowly in small increments while feeling the awareness in that sliver of your body. Yes, this takes patience particularly in the beginning. With diligence it does get easier and I know of people popping into non dual states with this method for what it's worth.

3) Interesting you mention this, as I recently stumbled across this technique and have been incorporating it into the "relaxation" phase at the beginning of my daily meditations in a fairly abbreviated form. It certainly has seemed to have some noticeable results, but I haven't found much more bodily awareness to be among them just yet. I might just need to do it for longer, either (or both) per session or for more sessions, as I only spend a minute or two on it and have only been at it for a few months. Thank you for this as well!

Thanks again,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
replies.content:
Some nice ideas for awareness exercises here!

It seems to me that the placement of our awareness is heavily influenced by our perception of our 'self'--what it is, what it does, as others here have said as well. When I think of having my consciousness only behind my eyes, the image/feeling that comes to me is looking out of a mask, or a paper bag placed over my head with holes for eyes. It gives the impression that "I" exist mainly as an observer, not a full-sensory participator in Midgard life. The other physical and metaphysical senses feel muffled in this thought-experiment.

One approach among others that might be helpful is choosing one other body area to work with next, not try to do many areas at once. I would recommend the heart as another logical place for a sense of Being and Awareness. In my article "Heathen Contemplation: The Resonance of the Heart" I write about the heart as an organ of perception and communication, a sensory organ that operates on many different levels of being. This viewpoint is supported by many scientific studies that show that the hearts of two people who feel close to each other (e.g. long-time couples, or mothers and their babies) tend to beat in unison when they are nearby to each other. At the end of my article I list descriptions and references to some of this research, and many more accounts can be found online.

What is it that causes this mutual resonance of heartbeats between people who are close to each other? Whatever the scientific viewpoints might be (it seems to me that quantum entanglement may play a role), I interpret this phenomenon as evidence that the heart, among its multitude of talents, can serve as a sensory organ and an organ of communication. This communication consists of resonances or vibrations that the heart sends out, picks up from others, and interprets: it is able to find great meaning within these resonances, life-shaping meaningfulness. Consider that all our sensory organs are also modes of communication: meaningful glances of the eyes, speech communicating through mouth and hearing, hugs or blows reaching us through skin-sensation, etc. With the heart, sensing and communicating take place through resonance--electromagnetic resonance on the physical level, and other forms on other levels.

Perhaps this would be a good area for you to work with next, Jeff, in placing your self-perception otherwhere than your eyes. I would think that your Heathen rosary would be the ideal context for such work, since I believe that resonance is a fundamental mode of communication and mutually shared experience with the Deities. Plus, your rosary is beautiful and powerful, and the heart responds eagerly to both! You may be able to sense your own heart-energy expanding out of your body, and be able to place your self-awareness within that field of energy.

As a more silly kind of suggestion, perhaps you could imagine yourself as the Greek demigod Argos the Watchman, who had 100 eyes all over his body! That would extend your eyes-self connection considerably! :-D

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
replies.content:
Hi Jeff and all!
Where attention goes energy flows is a hatha yoga maxim, and the prior expressed techniques of breathing into and moving where you want to send your perceptive abilities is sound advice. To refine it a bit, maybe postures specific to the areas might help (like twisting poses for navel, backbends for heart, standing balances for feet etc.)
Each step of awareness practice from gross body to more subtle breath should slowly and carefully build and cultivate more refined perceptive abilities.
A rather extreme method and one that should be very carefully and slowly cultivated is to imagine yourself without a head. I hesitate to even suggest it as undisciplined results would obviously be awkward in everyday life🙃 but maybe give it a brief try to start?
One more thought: sound tones can be potent tools to awaken perceptions - in Sanskrit there are seed mantras for various body areas, and certain musical instruments resonate with particular body parts too, like brass instruments for throat, piano for heart, drums for legs and pelvic floor etc.

Hope this is of help! Best to all
Jill C

replies.content: I worked briefly with some of the practices from the heartmath institute which were aimed at placing one's consciousness into the heart and thinking and acting from there. I struggled for a bit with moving my consciousness there, and I'm not sure I would say I ever felt mastery of the practice. The visualization that helped most was to imagine my body as a space ship (like the Enterprise or Millennium Falcon,) and then I would imagine that I was looking out from the bridge while in my head, then once I had a distinct feeling of being in my body rather than being my body, I would climb down a ladder from the deck into the reactor core of my heart, I'd try to hold that sensation of being in my chest and the ship still having a bridge and all the data from there, but me being primarily involved with and perceiving from the heart core. This worked reasonably well for me.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on How to Pray the Rosary and Get Results and "Consciousness in Part of Body" Exercises
replies.content:
Hi Jeff!

I am glad you found the rosary techniques helpful for your project. I also think its an interesting idea to link up a Mystery to your goal in what seems like a whole magical working, but with prayer instead of ritual as a means. I like to imagine the virgin standing before me or atop the altar or bringing an object or someone and saying the prayers to her. When I finish praying the thoughtform seems to light up a little more in my vision and I try to listen for the guidance there if I don't want to do a full path(mystery?)working on it. Sometimes I thank and dismiss the image and sometimes it decides ti stays there for a little while afterwards and I let it, it seems to sometimes do stuff in the altar.

Regarding the expanded method and having the right correspondence in the prayer. Could it work if instead of one rosary to Idun, there are a whole set of rosaries with prayers in the right meter for the appropriate deity of the intention?

You might find his An occultish guide to the rosary interesting, which is a free pdf on the thavma site.

As for perception. I can't do it fully but what helps me in shifting perception is trying to "walk" into that part of my body in a similar way to what walking to somewhere else to do the daily ritual astrally feels like. Say, from my bed to my usual practice space, while feeling the weight, field of vision, temperature changing as I do so.

Interestingly enough, I have done a similar thing to the "ring exercise" that anon mentioned above.

Hi Jeff,
I’m a tai chi practitioner, and in our style the hand form is taught with verbal instruction referencing the specific body part where the attention should reside at a given point. Usually this is the fulcrum of the martial arts application (for example the knuckles of the fist in a punch). comments.title: Applied Cryptid Radionics
comments.content:
Is there a difference between etheric energy from humans and etheric energy from other sources?

I've been re-reading your dreamwidth series on radionics that mentioned earth pipes for introducing life force to the ground for crop growth, Leyden jars for concentrating and storing life force, and devices such as the Hieronymus Machine that seem to be able to manipulate the life force's compatibility with various tasks.

The inner Weird Tales author in me notices that there are several classes of cryptids such as vampires, succubi, and even ordinary humans who feed on the life force of other people for various reasons. I'm wondering whether the various classes of energy pests are obligated to make a nuisance of themselves preying on people or whether a mad scientist might be able to do something like attach an earth pipe to a Hieronymus Machine, probably in the presence of a standing stone to introduce the solar force to the area and guard against depletion, and let well behaved energy pests feed off of the resultant life energy in return for good behavior.

I've worked really well with venus in the past and would usually offer the orphyic hymn and candle on 6 consecutive Fridays at the hour of venus, followed by prayer and thanks. However, for reasons out of my control, I can't commit to 6 week straight at the moment. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Venus is hard to offend as long as you're honest with her. Let her know that you're going to do the working on seven Fridays but you won't be able to make them consecutive, and she'll be fine with it. BTW, 7 is the Venus number, so doing it 7 times would be best.

replies.title: Q
replies.content: 7 is a pretty holy number in different traditions. Is there a reason why it is associated with venus?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: You'd have to ask the ancient Mesopotamian astrologer-priests who assigned numbers to the planets, and to the best of my knowledge, they're not taking questions any more. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: One possibility is that the planetary numbers derive from the planetary "magic squares."  The smallest possible such square is 3x3.  If you assign it to the outermost ancient planet, Saturn, and then move inwards toward the earth (at the center), the per-Copernican sequence of planets and their associated squares is as follows: Saturn (3x3), Jupiter (4x4), Mars (5x5), Sun (6x6), Venus (7x7), Mercury (8x8) and Moon (9x9).  In this schema, the number of Venus is 7.

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List

comments.title: Peanut the squirrel
comments.content:
Okay JMG, I hope you agree this question is appropriate for Magic Monday, first the backstory:

If you haven’t heard of Peanut the Squirrel and Fred the Raccoon, you’ve been under a rock for the last 24 hours.

Peanut was rescued at age 5 weeks when his mother was killed by a car. A man named Mark Luongo, who now runs a licensed animal rescue shelter in NY state, saved and nursed him. Mark eventually tried to re-introduce Peanut back to the wild, but Peanut couldn’t hack it, so Mark took Peanut into his home as a house pet where he thrived. (I understand a similar story applies for Fred the Raccoon). Peanut became an internet sensation, with an Instagram account of seven million (?!?!) followers, becoming a mascot who served as a fund-raising dynamo for the animal shelter. There are numerous vids of Peanut’s playful, affectionate antics; they are really touching. This went on for 7 years!

Someone anonymously reported Peanut to health authorities (I’m not clear to me whether it was NY state authorities, Federal authorities, or both), on the grounds that the animals are rabies vectors, so a team of 6-10 agents, a SWAT team I gather, raided Mr. Luongo’s house for 5 hours, seized those pets and euthanized them to “test for rabies”. During the raid, officers demanded to see the immigration papers for Mr. Luongo’s wife!

The internet has gone ballistic with outrage. E.g. it’s made headlines on Zerohedge, see here:

https://www.zerohedge.com/political/rip-my-best-friend-outrage-ensues-after-beloved-rescue-squirrel-seized-ny-euthanized

and that’s just for openers.

This story has gone more viral than anything I can recall.

What I find odd is how this ties in to (and even flips?) attitudes about Trump vs the Democratic Party. Here is a sample of commentary:

replies.title: Re: Peanut the squirrel
replies.content:
"Voting nemesis over hubris". That seems like a pretty pithy epigraph.

I went to idly search that phrase and turned up an article in the LA Times from 1986, by the (later?) RAND analyst David Ronfeldt, warning of the phenomenon of the "hubris-nemesis complex".

https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1986-01-20-me-30982-story.html

> Castro, Kadafi and Khomeini have shown themselves to be filled with hubris, and perhaps they, too, will eventually bring about their own downfall. But that is not the point here.
>
> These three leaders embody the spread of a terrible new dynamic that we are only beginning to understand: the hubris-nemesis complex. In addition to having hubris, they have internalized the role of Nemesis. Each is committed to being the nemesis of the United States; for these men, America is the epitome of hubris, and hence the chosen enemy.
>
> In this rare pathology, the two forces, which normally contradict one another, have become one, a fusion that generates awesome energy and ambition. To be as powerful as their hubris requires, these leaders must defy and attack the United States. To play the role of Nemesis, they must possess total power at home and project themselves onto the world stage. The two forces, now compatible, feed on one another.
>
> [...]
>
> Ideology and evil are not the defining issues here. Ideology is a secondary motivation, if not a mask. And it does not stand to reason that somebody like Castro is simply evil-minded. He, like others, may define good and evil in absolute, polarizing terms, yet he may not see the chosen enemy as pure evil incarnate. In this complex, the desire to humiliate the enemy derives more from its perceived hubris (the way it demands or exercises power) than from its perceived evilness.

That last subtlety is something I didn't catch until I re-read more closely.

In http://twotheories.blogspot.com/2017/01/reason-1-trumps-psyche-hubris-nemesis.html from 2017, Ronfeldt gives some more analysis, applying the complex to Trump.

> In the classic dynamic, then, the two forces stand apart, opposing and contradicting each other. In this extraordinary dynamic, the two forces no longer stand apart. They get fused in a single psyche. They become compatible contradictions — mutually reinforcing each other in a fusion that, far from destroying the bearer, imparts enormous invigorating energy, ambition, dynamism, and charisma, along with a thirst for absolute power. The two forces feed on each other such that the stronger one is, the stronger the other may become as well.
>
> The mentality and behavior of a leader under the spell of both forces will be substantially different from those of a leader affected by only one or neither of the two forces. A hubris-nemesis complex seems to impart a rationality that differs from a conventional cost-benefit rationality. A leader inflated with that much grandiosity and vengefulness tends to believe that he or she is above the law. They may not make what are normally regarded as reasonable pragmatic calculations[...]

I guess Ronfeldt thought this was significant partly because he was working at RAND on international relations, and he noticed a pattern where the designated targets of the Nemeses would keep making consistent miscalculations about the motives of the bearer of the complex and what incentives they would be responsive to.

His proper writeup for RAND was in 1993:

https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/monograph_reports/2005/MR461.pdf

The LA Times article also discusses of how the US was in danger of this towards the USSR. But neither discussion describes the possibility of a full two-way manifestation of the complex, that we seem to be drifting toward now. (Maybe the RAND report does. It's long and I only glanced at it.)

I guess that's sort of related to the discussion of the Piscean Age.

comments.title: On the Design Of Internet Iconography
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm planning to expand my Internet presence to spread the word about my creative projects. Part of this process is designing logos and other branding graphics, to be used as avatars on various sites. Is there a divination approach you think would be particularly effective at determining the quality of the resonance a given image has with my intentions? My intention is for the chosen images to help draw the attention of interested people and to clarify the ideas I'm communicating. Choosing the day and hour of Mercury to do the work is one obvious step.

And a second, related question. I've heard from a few places commentary about the differing tastes of people on the political left and right when it comes to Internet personae. The general gist is that left-wing people tend to choose avatars and images that are intended to represent their identities or their desired identities, while right-wing people tend to choose a persona that represents some abstract idea they feel an affinity for. There's been considerable discussion of the psychological dimensions of these choices, but what do you see as the astral and energetic repercussions of people choosing to represent themselves in these ways?

For example, people in the transgender and/or furry communities, which skew heavily left-wing, have a strong tendency to create a character to represent themselves, commission artwork of the character and use it as their avatar in every possible venue. Others who aren't so dedicated may choose one or more characters from popular culture and use their images as avatars. Left-wing people who aren't so strongly tied to media fandoms still often seem to want to project a strong, consistent sense of identity when online, like with AI caricatures of themselves.

People on the right seem more likely to use something like an image of Rome burning or a planet that resembles a clown head (for "clown world") as their avatar on social media, and also to use a variety of images at different times and places depending on what they're currently thinking about as opposed to carefully maintaining a consistent persona. It seems to me that these tendencies reflect on people associating with and drawing power from egregores in different ways. Is there anything you see as particularly notable about these two types of energetic association?

Thanks as usual for these posts.

replies.title: Re: On the Design Of Internet Iconography
replies.content:
1) There are several ways you could go about this. On the one hand, you could consult your astrological chart, find the planet that is best dignified, and choose a logo that riffs off the symbolism of that planet to tap into the good fortune brought by that placement. Dreams and scrying can also be useful here.

2) Well, the thing that comes first to mind is that people who are trying to project a single, consistent, and false image of themselves in public are deeply conflicted about their actual identity, while people who are comfortable with diverse self-presentations are less stressed about who they are and how they behave. The fixation on a single image is a good way to see to it that the opposite of that image manifests in one's life, while a diverse choice of images avoids that.

replies.title: Re: On the Design Of Internet Iconography
replies.content: Do you think that someone heavily investing themself in a fictional image to use as a public persona is making themselves vulnerable to curses? Such character avatars could be defiled in a variety of ways, including purely mundane mockery. It reminds me of how publicized chaos magic sigils are vulnerable to counterspells that riff on the meaning of the letters. The subcultures that encourage the use of avatar characters also seem to tend to encourage weakness and pliability in their members.

replies.title: Re: On the Design Of Internet Iconography
replies.content: Possibly, yes. Let me guess: the avatars are usually characters who are strong and firm-willed, right? Thus the flight to the opposite, in which weakness and pliability come to the fore.

replies.content: The emphasis in these character designs is usually more on the side of being extremely cute and lovable, and as you might guess the people investing themselves heavily in these images tend to not be so attractive in real life.

replies.content: Yes, I've noticed that pretty reliably.

replies.title: Re: Bible
replies.content:
Across all of Christendom there are actually a fair number of different Bibles, each with its own unique "table of contents" or list of books that make up their Bible. (The technical term for a table of contents of a Bible is its "Canon of Scripture.")

So there are distinct Protestant and Catholic Bibles in Western Europe and its colonies; Greek Orthodox Bibles, Slavonic Orthodox Bibles and Georgian Orthodox Bibles in the Orthodox Churches; Coptic Bibles and Ethiopic Bibles in non-Chalcdonian Africa; and finally, Armenian Bibles and Syriac Bibles in the non-Chalcedonian Near East. Each of these Bibles has its own distinct Canon of Scripture, which is not without some influence on the esotericism of the community that uses it. (And of course there is also the Jewish Bible in Hebrew, and its Samaritan variant, also in Hebrew.)

And when you get into the really old manuscripts of one or another of these distinct Bibles, you find that individual manuscripts have somewhat different tables of contents from one another, too.

So the question is far more convoluted than one might suppose.

For English speakers, the King James Bible with the Apocrypha comes closest to what Medieval and Renaissance esotericists in Western Europe knew and used. It doesn't have quite the same Canon of Scripture as current (post-1500s) Catholic Bibles do, much less that of Protestant Bibles.

replies.title: Re: Bible
replies.content:
Hi there,

Some years ago I began a project of going through the Gospels starting with Matthew from an esoteric standpoint. You might find the posts interesting:

https://readoldthings.dreamwidth.org/tag/gospel+of+matthew

You'll notice I left off at Matthew 12, but I'll return to it eventually. Or you can write your own version-- I found the whole process to be excellent training in Occult philosophy.

As for versions of the Bible, though, though, what I would recommend is to not use a single version. Instead, use a range of versions-- they're all online at biblegateway.org. And you should also use online resources to find the original meaning of words. Among particular translations, the Revised Standard Edition is considered to be very accurate and it's also readable, and I usually use it as a reference. The King James Version has a long history of use in magic as well as English literature, and so is very worth careful study. St Jerome's Latin Vulgate is very easy to read if you have a basic knowledge of Latin-- and reading it will also expand your knowledge of the language, which will help with what JMG would call the development of your mental body. (If that sounds difficult, don't be intimidated--my Latin is at "Finds Caesar difficult and Ficino incomprehensible," but I can read the Vulgate without a dictionary.)

replies.title: Re: Bible
replies.content:
Not directly occult, but the New Revised Standard Version (NRSV) typically comes with some pretty informative footnotes discussing translation choices and comparisons with other versions. The version I have is the Harper Collins Study Bible, which is excellent on that front, and includes most (all?) of the Apocrypha and Pseduoepigraphia. Of course, as our host and others have said, if you're a native English speaker, the King James version has certain poetic resonances that other versions just can't touch.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Bible
replies.content:
Conan Doyle's spiritualist stuff is well-written. Since he was singing Christian hymns before inviting sprits, it's about as Christian as you can get. He was doing a Christian's Imitation of Christ as he tried to imitate what Jesus was doing in Galilee.

'The History of Spiritualism', 'The New Revelation' 'The Edge of the Unknown'.


He never forgot that could all be nonsense, and kept a very skeptical mind, as well as a batty trust in A Briton's Pledged Word' and a wildly pash for dramatic improbability. His claim that Houdini was turning into ectoplasm to escape being shackled and dropped in rivers, say. Or the photos of fairies in Yorkshire.

He took 'ectoplasm' seriously, having seen it in person repeatedly in seances. I think in part because he'd been on a deep-sea exploring trip under a mad scientist who dredged up strange glowing goo from the sea bottom. In the 1880s and throughout Doyle's life with was seriously considered some kind of basic protolife. (Nowadays it's just considered fat deposited by clumps of algae. Pity.)

comments.content:
I’ve wondered for a while given how closely the endocrine glands seem to be related to the energy centres, if it is possible to attain different spiritual states by deliberately altering hormones, and if some of the weird lifestyles that certain spiritual paths require or encourage are intentional efforts to adjust hormone levels (admittedly, likely without always knowing that this is why the practices work). I think I might have found one of these: namely, a lot of the habits of monks around the world seem to be designed to force insulin levels down to unnaturally low levels.

The reason I’m looking into this is because I recently realized I was likely either diabetic or fast heading that way, and decided I wanted to use a set of lifestyle changes to fix things rather than deal with the medical system. Type II diabetes, the form I was developing, is a disease with a pretty well understood cause: insulin is forced up to such unnaturally high levels for long enough that the body loses the ability to function properly. Certain tissues stop responding properly, which forces the hormone level up further, which causes a host of issues if that positive feedback loop is not broken. The obvious solution then was to use lifestyle changes to force insulin down, hard, in order to give my body a chance to bring insulin levels back into the normal range. I was surprised at how much of that was essentially “Live like a monk”.

Insulin is secreted mostly in response to blood sugar, which can be forced down by fasting, both over the course of a day and over longer time periods, and by eating smaller, lighter meals. The habit in many monasteries of eating only once or twice a day, and also the habit of regular extended fasting is in fact one of the major ways known to hack insulin and push it down. Insulin levels also depend on sensitivity of liver tissues and muscles, and thus on how much food is being eaten in one sitting (which the liver has to process) and the amount of moderate aerobic exercise (which causes muscles to burn glucose they then replenish when given the chance). The common European monastic habits of doing hard physical labor, or the Indian monastics who walk from city to city, deplete the muscles, and make them far more sensitive to insulin. Avoiding sweet foods, especially paired with simple, lighter meals, another common monastic habit also keeps insulin down by means of keeping pulses of food, especially sugar, out of the system, thus reducing how high it ever needs to go, a key factor in keeping the liver able to process blood sugar.

The liver is also affected by essentially all mind altering substances, and cannot absorb nearly as much sugar when it is breaking them down, and thus the near universal monastic habit of limiting them makes perfect sense in this light; while two which are commonly used as spiritual aids, caffeine and nicotine, both push insulin down despite this: caffeine by boosting metabolism, nicotine by suppressing appetite enough to reduce reduce either food intake or the normal cues to conserve energy, or in some cases both. (Tobacco was marketed as a weight loss drug for quite a while as a result)

While it does not contain much sugar as a general rule, meat also triggers insulin, because it contains certain amino acids that that the body uses as a cue for protein consumption; with protein being converted into sugar when consumed in levels beyond the modest amount needed for protein synthesis, this insulin trigger helps manage blood sugar levels when the protein is converted into glucose. Vegetarian sources of protein do not contain these in the same quantities, and so they do not trigger nearly as strong an insulin response. Once more, monasteries all over the world tend to encourage vegetarian diets, no matter what the normal diet of their community is.

There are also at least a half dozen other common monastic habits (ranging from exposure to the elements to waking before dawn for prayer) that seem designed to help with lowering insulin levels; but at this point this is already quite a long comment, so I'm going to leave them out.

a) Do you think it’s possible that there is something to this idea of monkish habits being a means of accessing certain spiritual states by means of intentionally pushing insulin down to unusually low levels?

b) If forcing insulin down to unnaturally low levels is part of what makes monastic environments so powerful for spirituality, then do you think it’s also possible that part of the reason that Americans seem so spiritually dull compared to other societies, both historically and in other nations, might be related to this? If I wanted to force insulin up as high as possible I’m not sure I could do a better job than the standard American lifestyle; it’s a strong enough trend, and this sort of high insulin is damaging enough to human health, I’ve come to suspect it might actually be intentional as a means of ensuring profits for the medical industry...

In a recent comment you posted a link to your ADR posts on religious sensibility. I haven't read all of them yet, but it helped me to realize that a culture's core assumptions shape their religious beliefs and practices, which one might otherwise believe are based on a absolute, fixed 'truth'. You used the example of how religions like Christianity and Buddhism are focused on salvation, which is due to their own religious sensibility.

At the beginning of Dion Fortune's The Training and Work of an Initiate, she stated that the goal of western occultism is union with the Higher Self. Do you think that statement is more to do with her own Christian beliefs, and/or that western occultism is also focused on salvation in its own way? Or is the work of union with the Higher Self something overarching, and that a civilization's religious sensibility is just their own particular way of handling that work or how it thinks about that work?

The reason I ask is because the Higher Self seems like it is anatomical, you have a Higher Self in the same way that you have a mouth or arms, and so it isn't an idea to agree with or not. Hm, have I just answered my own question?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The religious sensibility that runs through modern Western occultism does in fact have union with the Higher Self, variously phrased and understood, as its central theme. It's different in important ways from other religious sensibilities -- for example, to the Buddhist there is no higher self, and getting rid of the illusion that the self exists at all is essential to salvation; to the Christian some form of union ofr the ordinary self with God, whether that's seen as dissolution of the self into the divine (as many mystics believe) or a spiritual marriage of the soul with Christ, is the goal of salvation.

The occult vision differs from these. To the occultist the personality we experience in our everyday lives is a veil that covers our real identity, and that real identity -- the Higher Self -- participates in a community of spiritual beings that centers on, and is in constant relationship with, the Divine. It's a very distinctive religious sensibility, not really like anything else out there.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, in at least some cases. In Roman Pagan religion there was the concept of the genius or juno -- the former for men, the latter for women -- a guardian spirit who was seen as the distributor of good or bad fortune to the individual. It was standard to make offerings to one's genius or juno on one's birthday, to thank them for another year of life and ask for good fortune for the year ahead.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings!

You had said this recently:

"From within the occult teachings I mostly work with, the elementals and nature spirits are "creations of the created," in Dion Fortune's phrase -- they were created by the soul-swarms before ours (the elementals by the Lords of Flame, the nature spirits by the Lords of Form) and have no direct contact with the Divine. One of the tasks of our soul-swarm, at and above the human level, is to reintegrate the elementals and make it possible for them to evolve; that's why some initiatory traditions (Rosicrucian and Druid in particular) have close relationships with the elemental kingdoms and work with them in magic and other forms of operative occultism. The reintegration of the nature spirits will be the work of a soul-swarm following ours."

1) Could you discuss this more? How and why were they created, what their roles are etc?

2) Is it possible that we are similarly trying to create some type of artificial machine intelligence? Curious what your thoughts are on that.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) The history of the solar system, from an occult point of view, is the history of seven swarms of souls, of which we're the fourth. The first, which became the Lords of Flame once they finished their evolution, turned the solar system from a cloud of dust to a collection of planets; in the process, they made the elementals, who maintain what scientists call the laws of astronomy and physics, and keep the solar system running at all scales, from the microscopic on up. Once that was done, and the Lords of Flame settled into their role as angelic beings, the second swarm got started, and got the planets (which the Lords of Flame left as blobs of white-hot molten rock) to something like their current state; they also got various kinds of life going in planets suited for them -- ours is supposed to be the only planet with chemical life, but some of the other planets have different forms of life. The nature spirits came into being in this process, and they maintain what scientists call the laws of chemistry and biology. The third swarm, which became the Lords of Mind, was responsible for multicellular evolution, and our swarm, which will become the Lords of Freedom, is responsible for the awakening of individual intelligence in living things. We can't even imagine what the three swarms that come after us will do.

2) In a crude clumsy way, sure. Our main work, as members of our swarm of souls, consists of getting animal forms to the point of being able to handle abstract thinking. We spent a long time making that happen with a few species, notably Homo sapiens, and we're now hard at work spreading it -- that's why we keep pets, for example. The sense I get is that when our swarm has finished its work on the material plane, many millions of years from now, most animals will be as intelligent as humans are now, either individually or in collectives (for example, beehives and anthills are working their way toward becoming intelligent individuals, in which each bee or ant is simply a cell or component. Our culture has this cute little fixation with machines, which we're in the process of outgrowing; I'm sure a million years from now nobody will even remember that.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Thank you.

1) So our jobs is primarily to awaken "freedom" in a sense in already existing creations, rather than create a new species like the swarms before us?

2) Do you see a possibility for souls or consciouness to be born into a machine or partially machine-partially biological substrate from the scientific developments of humanity over the its time on earth?

Or even to use these developments to foster abstract thinking in animals?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) There are always new species coming into existence and old ones going out of existence. The first three swarms had to create entire categories of beings -- elementals, nature spirits, living organisms, plants, animals, etc. Our task, according to the lore, is to take these existing categories of beings and start the process by which they will rise to an intelligence that, ultimately, will transcend ours.

2) No, because that assumes that human beings will keep progressing technologically, and we won't. Our culture has taken technology about as far as it can go, and we've burnt through a billion years of stored fossil fuels and a fantastic range of other irreplaceable natural resources to do it. Our descendants won't be able to maintain what we've created, much less build further on it; they will go in their own directions, and my guess is that they'll focus on the mind and spirit to a much greater extent than we ever did.

replies.title: Re: Dinosaurs
replies.content: Were the dinosaurs of the same swarm as us?

replies.title: Re: Dinosaurs
replies.content: Yes. They weren't that long ago, you know -- the Mesozoic ended only 66 million years ago, and lasted almost three times that (186 million years).

replies.title: Re: Dinosaurs
replies.content: I see. So we - meaning, the current swarm - must be at the end of its run on the material plane, right?

replies.title: Re: Dinosaurs
replies.content: Good heavens, no. We've only been here a few hundred million years, and we have a lot of work still to do. Individual souls in the swarm will finish their time in matter and rise to more interesting tasks, but I figure we have hundreds of millions of years to go before our swarm has finished its work and the next swarm can pick things up.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: You'd suggested that there would be a future high technology civilization again (ruled by jupiter?)? Perhaps not using fossil fuels?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: I've suggested -- my book The Ecotechnic Future is all about this -- that there will be many societies in the future that will be, as I call them, technic societies; that is to say, societies that derive a fair amount of their energy supply from something other than human and animal muscle. Those societies will be very different from ours; they will quite possibly have scientific knowledge in advance of ours, but because they won't have vast amounts of cheap accessible fossil fuels. they'll have to be much less stupidly wasteful with their energy resources than we've been. Thus they almost certainly won't have highways, airliners, and spacecraft. What technologies will they have? We almost certainly can't imagine, any more than an ancient Sumerian could predict our technologies.

My husband and I have both been sensing a malicious, incorporeal entity periodically around our home for the last several years. We have only felt it at night, after bedtime, until (maybe) yesterday. Yesterday evening after supper we were discussing the entity and what to do about it. We suspect that it might be involved in a persistent state of pessimism, negative emotion, and volatile mood that my husband has been experiencing. comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Polytheist Pagans in the Piscean Era
replies.content:
That explains so much!

I get weird "nostalgia" moments looking at photos of old historic parts of Chinese cities. When I was a little kid I had a few flashes of imagery of a bustling city with Medieval Chinese-style architecture, with the cityscape being crisscrossed by narrow canals. And for some odd reason, I enjoy reading Mahayana Buddhist sutras, despite not (on the intellectual level) finding the core doctrines of Buddhism to be at all congenial. replies.content: Fair enough! replies.title: Re: Polytheist Pagans in the Piscean Era
replies.content:
As a polytheist I can certainly identify with these thoughts! But...

There are a great many religions, cultures, and places around the world where polytheistic beliefs existed up to the very recent past and into the present, and where the souls of those of us attracted to polytheism could have hung out prior to this life.

Even in Europe, northern Europe took much longer for Christianization to take place than the Mediterranean area did. Lithuania was the last European country to be 'officially' Christianized, that is, for its king to officially proclaim that the country was Christianized, in 1387. That's a roughly 600-year hiatus, not 2000 years!

Laws, edicts, church ordinances, etc, against pagan beliefs and practices continued to be enforced long after European countries were 'officially' Christian, indicating that there were numerous people who followed those beliefs in some form. European folklore shows a great many folk-practices that assume some sort of pagan and polytheistic perspective: Jacob Grimm's massive "Teutonic Mythology" offers a wealth of examples, and there are many others such as Irish, Breton, Slavic, and other folk-beliefs.

Major branches of Christianity offer the saints as subjects of religious practice, and I suspect that people subtly drawn to polytheism would find this a satisfying alternative. Even more so was, and is, the veneration of Mary the mother of Jesus. That was one of the reasons for wars of the Protestants against the Catholics: because the Protestants considered veneration of the saints to be polytheistic. So, polytheistic souls in Christian cultures of the past could have just been especially devoted to Mary and the saints, which a great many people were. Some of the Christian saints simply took over the roles of pagan deities, and if you look at folklore you can see a great many practices consistent with pagan belief.

Finally, I don't know that the energies of the Piscean Age necessarily enforce monotheism. Piscean energies can be expressed through polytheistic religions and practices, too. For example, ahimsa and other Hindu and Buddhist movements toward 'no-harm,' including vegan eating, and the practices of the Jains and others. (Though ahimsa started before the Piscean age, but expanded during it.) Those religions don't generally express as monotheistic, though dogmatism is certainly present!

Presumably the whole world has been subject to Piscean energies for the past 2000 years, but not all people and cultures of the world have been monotheistic during this time, so monotheism might not be a defining feature of Piscean energies.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Polytheist Pagans in the Piscean Era
replies.content: Thanks so much for this Winifred! That makes a lot of sense to me, that even if I were in Europe I could have been in a place with lingering pagan folk beliefs. I could see me doing something like venerating Mother Mary also as the Earth Mother, or basically bringing the archangels in as a form of quasi-polytheism. I'm noting down Grimm's book to investigate.

comments.title: The Odd ‘Last President’ Prophecy?
comments.content:
Not so much a question but a rumination. In recent weeks I have chanced upon several videos which describe the same thing: the oddly prophetic fictional works by an obscure late 19th century author named Ingersoll Lockwood. Written in the first person by one Baron Trump, the series describes how Baron lives in ‘Trump Castle’ in downtown New York and is son of a big businessman who becomes president of the USA as a brave and brilliant populist outsider in a time of political corruption and chaos. In the work ‘The Last President’ the aftermath of the election includes the corrupt elite deliberately destroying the nation’s economy and, ultimately, the break-up of the USA while Trump is president. By the looks of things, Lockwood was a keen observer of the nature of politics and the ploys of the powerful.

Summaries of these books by Lockwood in the 1890s bring up an extraordinary number of details that are pretty close to the reality of the 2020s, including names, places, and circumstances. Kind of reminds me of Morgan Robertson’s work ‘Futility, Or The Wreck of the Titan’ which in 1912 described the Titanic in uncannily accurate detail before it was constructed. Makes me ponder on how sometimes authors can unwittingly tune into the currents of future events with far greater accuracy than the best-trained and gifted psychics.

For the record, I certainly hope that Lockwood’s description of what happens after Trump’s election is dead wrong. May tomorrow's US election be fair, decisive, and result in peace, harmony and prosperity.

Ron M

comments.title: Piscean Age
comments.content:
Another post here that I responded to makes me ask this question, or rather pose this confusion of mine, for your response & that of the commentariat, JMG.

People speak of the past 2000 years or so as the Piscean Age, characterized by Piscean traits. Yet it seems to me that this characterization depends a lot on where one stands to make that characterization. It sounds rather like the Christian characterization of itself, and perhaps the Confucian one as well--another philosophy that developed during the Piscean Age. Many non-Christian cultures and people, as well as those who were considered heretics, collectively or singularly, I think did not experience Christian culture and history as primarily Piscean.

What about places and peoples subject to colonialism, going back to the beginning of the Piscean era with Roman and other forms of colonization, and up to the present time? The ages of forcible conversions and colonizations: Christian, Muslim, Mughal, Hunnic, even Buddhist in some times and places, seem to me more Arean than Piscean. The African continent had its own tumultuous histories during this time, likewise the Americas, and what is known (to me, anyway) of their histories doesn't seem especially Piscean except perhaps in terms of creativity. It's certainly true that great art and discoveries happened during this time, but not only during this time.

So I just wonder what you all perceive about the Piscean nature of the Piscean age, that makes it Piscean? Do you find that it appears to be Piscean no matter whose shoes one stands in, or do some shoes fit the Piscean age more than others, so to speak?

Thank you for your thoughts!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Piscean Age
replies.content:
The astrological ages, as I see them, are a little more subtle than they're sometimes thought to be. There have been conquests and colonizations in every age, for example. The distinguishing features of the Piscean age are primarily seen in religions, and these have two very distinctive features. The first is that they focus on belief -- "I believe" is the classic two-word summary of Sun in Pisces -- where faiths in other ages weren't especially concerned with what opinions you happened to have. In Piscean faiths -- Christianity, Islam, Buddhism, and more -- believing in The Right Teaching is the thing that matters. Buddhas preach the dharma, Christians preach the gospel, and so on -- it's all about telling people what opinions they ought to hold. That's very Piscean.

The other is a habit of hard moral dualism. To the Piscean mind, the universe is divided into good and evil; evil has an independent existence, and it can be gotten rid of once and for all by the right religion. There's always some one cause for evil -- ignorance in Buddhism, original sin in Christianity, and so on -- and there's always the hope that, individually or collectively, we can become completely free from evil. Of course it never works out that way; good and evil are the two fish of Pisces, swimming in opposite directions but inseparably connected. But the craving to break the connection is also very Piscean.

replies.title: Re: Piscean Age
replies.content:
That's very helpful, thank you! From that perspective, one might even see the brutal Aztec sacrificial practices as Piscean, for example: believing that human sacrifice protects the world from evil consequences, and it has to be done right! Likewise the brutalities of the Inquisition and religious conversions, and colonialism with the "White Man's Burden" of civilizing and 'saving' people 'the right way.' Same with the belief in 'Manifest Destiny' with the Euro-American expansion into the American West. And the Ghost Dance movement among some Native American tribes, and similar politico-mystical movements in some African regions in reaction to colonialism, and on and on... Yep, I can see it, alright--thanks!

Winifred

comments.title: Magicking dogs, and so forth
comments.content:
Recently, I was waiting in line at the pharmacy to pick up my meds, and I noticed that the guy in front of me had a dog on a leash, who was resting quite serenely on the carpeting. So, I tried an experiment -- I conjured up feelings of positivity and goodwill, bound them together, and sent them to the dog, an imaginary heart floating from me to him. Then, 5-10 seconds later, he sat up, startled, and turned and stared at me rather intently. No barks, wags, nothing else, just his gaze fixated on me for the next couple minutes, until he left with his owner. Looking at him or away from him didn't seem to make a difference, I didn't interact with the dog in any other way, and he had ignored me before that.

It's apparent that I pulled off magic, and the dog knew what I was up to on some level. This leads to various questions:

What sort of magic did I do there? I assume astral or etheric? As I've said, I'm an amateur at this stuff, so I just did what came naturally.
Does this indicate significant magical ability, or can anyone with limited knowledge/experience pull this off? For reference, this was my most significant magical act since my first working a year & a half ago or so.
Are dogs (and other pets) particularly susceptible to magical interaction?
Is it okay to do things like this? I feel like I was basically petting the dog, just in a non-material sort of way.
Any other feedback, I'd be interested. I hope you don't mind the flurry of questions, I'm just trying to get a sense of this, since it's rather unprecedented for me personally.

replies.title: Re: Magicking dogs, and so forth
replies.content: Dogs are extremely sensitive to the nonphysical. So are cats -- if you've ever seen a cat staring intently at something you can't see, you may have guessed at that. The magic you did was astral -- if it's about feelings and imagery, it's astral by definition -- and the dog noticed it and was probably trying to figure out what you were up to. As for whether it was a good idea, probably not -- the dog didn't wag its tail or make any other sign of approval, so I don't recommend doing that again unless you get a dog of your own and it finds such things pleasant.

replies.title: Re: Magicking dogs, and so forth
replies.content:
Well, I didn't mean to offend him, I was just trying things out, and figured that positive energy wouldn't be a problem. But if it was out-of-bounds, then I'll respect that from now on. While he wasn't positive, he wasn't negative either (no growls etc); it was just a long stare, and when I saw him later in the parking lot, he seemed quite back to normal. I forgot to mention, earlier I had tried the same working with a bunny in my backyard, but I didn't seem to get a response (at least nothing apparent), which is why this stood out all the more.
This is yet more proof that magic is real, since there's really no other explanation, and will help me in further getting over my rationalist conditioning, so there's that at least.
I have noticed cats seeming to interact with invisible things -- that definitely explains it!
Anyway, I suppose I can develop my capacities without involving others for the time being.

replies.content:
I have some significant ups and downs with energy and willpower. They seem to be tied to lunar phases and also effected by weather patterns. I haven't found a way to store energy, but I have been learning how to work with the tides. I can now better feel when the energy is rising and I know that if I don't channel it productively it will often spill into anxiousness, worry, and then can lead to over indulgence and numbing activities (from media consumption to news obsession to overuse of alcohol and coffee.) On the other end, I know that when the energy wanes, I'm prone to overworking and staying up late trying to keep up productivity and then I burn out quickly and am again prone to movie watching, sugar overload, and overuse of caffeine to supplement the energy (which rarely ends well for me.)
So, I try to create and hold intentions when energy rises, and be productive while it's high, and then actually allow myself to rest more when it's low. It doesn't often mesh well with modern rat-race life that expects always-on responsiveness and has few margins. I'm currently working on reducing busy-ness where I can and increasing margins so that I can better flow with the energy tides. I've grown to appreciate religious teachings about having a day of rest (whether you want it or not) as taking a break each week helps lessen the peaks and troughs of my energy.

replies.title: Re: A question about ethereal energy
replies.content:
hearthculture said much of what I was going to share - I've been becoming more aware of my energy levels the last few years, and especially this year, and one key (but very hard) lesson, has been learning not to try to maintain high energy, but rather to ride the waves as they come and go.

That being said, I'd add a few other things to help reduce the variance between the peaks and troughs, and to a lesser extent, raise the midline. All the usual physical stuff: plenty of sleep at the right times (sleeping 10:00 pm to 6:00 am may do you more good than 12:00 - 8:00 am, or vice versa), eat healthy and enough (but not too much), regular moderate exercise in a blend of endurance and strength training that works for you (some folks thrive on lots of walking and almost no strength training, others by lifting heavy and barely ever running or walking), moderate or forego strongly energy-affecting substances or activities (caffeine, nicotine, social media, games, etc), cool or cold showers might charge you up, and the right amount of sun at the right times (exposure to sunlight soon after waking helps a lot of people, but some folks do better with gradual brightening versus stepping from a dark room into late morning sun).

Starting to get slightly more occult, you might consider some light daily breathing exercises. I've found that when I'm more consistent with these, my energy's a little more consistent, even if I don't always feel as much of a "boost" from the exercise as I would prefer (see above and hearthculture's comment about surfing the waves, though!). I say "light" daily breathing exercises, because this is something I've noticed can have a cumulative effect. Something that feels good once or twice might leave you a bit "wired" after doing it every day for a week or two. Likewise with some of the more esoterically-oriented physical exercises, like the Five Rites.

Lastly, also straddling the physical/occult boundary, and a bit more delicately, consider paying some attention to your sexual arousal and what you do about it. Our host has written in some detail about what you can expect depending on the configuration of your subtle bodies (https://bookshop.org/p/books/the-philosophy-and-practice-of-polarity-magic-a-secret-wisdom-of-sex-john-michael-greer/21072352?ean=9781801521482), but the short version is that your physical ability get aroused and your emotional/imaginal excitement about/craving for that arousal might not always match up with the etheric energy linked up with those activities. If you have a feminine etheric body (like most biological men), if you take care of such things yourself too often, you might end up drained and low energy, whereas if you engage with an etherically-polarized partner frequently, while you might feel physically relaxed or satisfied, you might end up energetically "full."

Anyhow, hope some of this helps, and good luck getting somewhere comfortable and productive for you.

Cheers,
Jeff

Focusing on the symbol of protection also means you wouldn't be focusing on the source of whatever nastiness is coming your way, potentially breaking any astral connection that might be active, and the technique can be done regardless of whether your real or astral bodies are mobile, so it may be useful in a variety of situations.

replies.title: Re: Astral possession through the back gates?
replies.content:
I have a thing like that, but my introspection says it's not so much that I don't have a functioning mirror system, as that I have one but it's not buffered in the ways it needs to be. Eye contact opens a kind of implied tacit negotiation feedback loop that goes by too fast (microexpressions are fast) and too intensely, and puts me too much on the back foot with respect to justifying important things that the other person doesn't already immediately emotionally understand. Sort of like a milder version of being in front of a mob. And the negotiation has this consistent "the medium is the message" side effect by its very nature of the intuitive "language" in which the tacit commitments it demands are cognitively formulated. The side effect creates this constant pernicious pressure to think about social-emotionally-colorable things with the particular kind of thinking entailed in the negotiation commitments, and this style of thinking is malefic when unrestrained -- kind of "wordcel" as opposed to "shape rotator". I guess a lot of the negotiation seems to bypass consciousness and get experienced merely as a disruptor, at least for me whose center of self-awareness isn't in my social thinking. Or at least it did back when I was learning habits about this sort of thing. It's hard to introspect usefully about this for some reason; this account is largely a tissue of guesswork.

To explain it to people coming from the other side, I guess the direction of effect here, if not the magnitude, could be analogized to the direction of effect you would expect from being mildly drugged into a dissociative compliant state and then "voluntarily" dumped under intense social pressure into a week-long intensive retreat where all interactions are conducted in bureaucratese, about everything. Where everything has to be triple-checked to avoid appearances of entangling informality and maintain CYA outward defensibility towards bored uninterested third-party auditors to whom there'd be no socially viable way to communicate the context of anything. You don't come away from that being able to relate to your previous priorities the same way, or even in some new, alternatively functional way: thinking that way makes you incrementally stop siding with people who actually understand contexts to see where normal procedures are ruinous (and it can even make you start siding with those who would profit from ruin, empowering them to vindictively act under a veneer of respectability and upholding the system). So you avoid it if you can.

The predisposing factors here, of an insufficiently buffered social brain, may have significant overlap with predisposing factors to borderline and avoidant personality disorders.

A significant observation is that if part of my face is covered, the eye contact suddenly stops being uncomfortable. This implies that the negotiation has something to do with rapid-feedback equilibria, that aren't the familiar conceptual recursive I-know-you-know-I-know-you-know... sort of thing, but an emotional recursive I-know-you-feel-X-about-knowing-I-feel-Y-about-knowing-you-feel-Z-about.... comments.title: Plato
comments.content:
I want to ask you about Plato and his importance to both the old and modern civilization. The more I read, the more it strikes me that he only of the most important individuals in history. I read somewhere that Blavatsky claimed that Plato's soul has so influential it hasn't reincarnated since, waiting for the right time.

1) Could you talk more about him and his influence, and how he was able to be so profoundly impactful, and how did his mysticism play a role in his theories?

2)What is your view on Spengler claiming Plato was the rational phase of Apollonian civilization, and even more so in the modern era, people like Leo Strauss and his followers who view Plato as purely a political or ethical thinker and are contemptuous of his mysticism, which they regard as "noble lies" generated for population compliance, or as mythical allegories? (this seems wrong to me btw).

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content:
1) Plato was crucial because of when and where he was, even more than who he was. Yes, he was an extremely insightful thinker, but he also happened along right as Greek philosophy was discovering the hard limits on how much the human mind can know about the world around it, and starting to turn more attention to the powers and potentials of the individual human being. His mysticism, which came to him from the Pythagoreans, was central to his teachings, but his legacy ended up divided between those who were receptive to the mystical dimension and those like Strauss who rejected it.

2) Plato's political thought is his weakest side. His ethics are better but still open to challenge -- the Stoics and Epicureans both got started out of critiques of the flaws of his ethical thought -- and his mysticism has shaped most mystical thought in the Western world since his time. That is to say, Strauss had his head so far up his nether regions that he was choking on his own hair. Plato was entirely rational -- but rationalism does not exclude mysticism. It's precisely because Plato was a rational mystic that he's been so difficult for modern thinkers to grasp, and such a rich source of insights for those who make the effort.

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content:
Thank you!

Would you know how his mysticism affect his political and ethical theories? Did he get ideas from the beyond etc? In general, what do you think is the relation between the spiritual world and our politics, including political innovation?

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content: That's a subject for a book, not a comment here! Actually, two books -- one on the interface between mysticism and the other dimensions of Plato's philosophy, and another, even larger, on the history of mystical ideas in politics. Neither of these are simple matters.

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content: What is your view on Plato's idea of Anamnesis? Is it similar to what happens after a kundalini awakening, or some other spiritual event correlate?

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Plato
replies.content: I would encourage anyone interested in Plato's "political" thought to engage with Edward Butler's book The City of the Gods, which is an analysis of the Republic and how it relates to the organization of its subject, which is the soul.

comments.title: Ageless or Ancient Widsom, definition
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have read several books within the Theosophical/Anthroposphical traditions, and I am still not clear on what the "Ageless Wisdom" or "Ancient Wisdom" refers to. Would you equate it with the Perennial Philosophy (aka Traditional School?)? I reckon it includes some of the ancient Vedic teachings and "mythologiies", the Puranas, etc. Sometimes it seems to me it's used as a "catch all" to lend credence or prestige to a specific belief or set of beliefs. Is it a definite body of work, does it have a traceable origin, and what criteria are there for what counts or doesn't count under this umbrella?

Relatedly, do you believe it is important in studying and evaluating esoteric/spiritual beliefs and claims to have some kind of grounding in a specific structure or system (e.g. theosophy, anthroposophy, Christianity, esoteric Buddhism, etc) or does such a systemic grounding/basis/reference point more often blind us to possible truths or realities which it cannot or does not accomodate?

Many Thanks for your thoughtful replies as always!

replies.title: Re: Ageless or Ancient Widsom, definition
replies.content:
1) "Ageless wisdom" is a brand name. So, for that matter, are "the perennial philosophy" and "Tradition." Spiritual traditions are anything but ageless or perennial, because human consciousness changes over time. Various people -- Theosophists and Anthroposophists among them -- have tried to market their particular (and peculiar) teachings as the one true ancient wisdom passed down since the dawn of time, but that claim is always either ignorant or dishonest. Theosophy is a particular philosophical-mystical movement, with a very idiosyncratic take on things; Anthroposophy is another. Each of them is valid in its own right and can take you far toward mystical attainment if you do the work, but the same is true of countless other systems.

2) It's impossible not to have a frame of reference. If you don't have a grounding in some specific esoteric tradition, you'll take your frame of reference unconsciously from pop culture or some equally unhelpful source. For that reason, I recommend a specific course of study. First, get a very solid grounding in the theory and practice of one sych system -- it doesn't matter in the least which one. Then, when you've achieved some level of mastery in that system, study several others. That will give you some breadth of knowledge and enable you to avoid the narrowing effect of knowing just one. What Goethe said of languages is just as true of esoteric spiritual systems: "He who knows only one, knows none."
